content:
# Acts Chapter 28 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 260-261 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_028_page_001.jpg, ACT_028_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And having been brought safely through, we then learned that the island was called Malta.

**2** And the native people showed us no ordinary kindness, for having kindled a fire, they received us all, on account of the rain that had set in and on account of the cold.

**3** Now when Paul had gathered a bundle of sticks and laid them upon the fire, a viper came out because of the heat and fastened itself upon his hand.

**4** And when the native people saw the creature hanging from his hand, they said to one another, "Surely this man is a murderer, whom, though he has been saved from the sea, Justice has not allowed to live."

**5** He, however, shook off the creature into the fire and suffered no harm.

**6** But they were expecting him to swell up or suddenly to fall down dead. And when they had waited a long time and saw nothing unusual happening to him, they changed their minds and said that he was a god.

**7** Now in the region around that place were lands belonging to the chief man of the island, named Publius, who received us and hospitably entertained us for three days.

**8** And it happened that the father of Publius lay sick with fever and dysentery. Paul went in to him, and having prayed and laid his hands on him, healed him.

**9** And when this had taken place, the rest of those on the island who had diseases also came and were healed.

**10** They also honored us with many honors, and when we were setting sail, they loaded us with the things we needed.

**11** And after three months we set sail in a ship that had wintered at the island, an Alexandrian ship, with the sign of the Dioscuri.

**12** And having put in at Syracuse, we remained there three days.

**13** From there we sailed around and arrived at Rhegium. And after one day a south wind sprang up, and on the second day we came to Puteoli,

**14** where we found brothers and were urged to remain with them seven days. And so we came to Rome.

**15** And from there the brothers, having heard about us, came to meet us as far as the Forum of Appius and the Three Taverns. When Paul saw them, he gave thanks to God and took courage.

**16** And when we entered Rome, Paul was permitted to stay by himself, with the soldier who was guarding him.

**17** And it came to pass that after three days he called together the leading men of the Jews. And when they had assembled, he said to them, "Brothers, though I had done nothing against our people or the customs of our fathers, I was delivered as a prisoner from Jerusalem into the hands of the Romans,

**18** who, having examined me, wished to release me, because there was no cause of death in me.

**19** But when the Jews objected, I was compelled to appeal to Caesar — not as though I had any accusation to bring against my own nation.

**20** For this reason, therefore, I have called for you, to see you and to speak with you, for it is on account of the hope of Israel that I am bound with this chain."

**21** And they said to him, "We have received no letters from Judea concerning you, nor has any of the brothers who arrived reported or spoken anything evil about you.

**22** But we think it fitting to hear from you what you think, for concerning this sect, it is known to us that everywhere it is spoken against."

**23** And having appointed a day for him, they came to him at his lodging in greater numbers. He expounded the matter to them, testifying to the kingdom of God and persuading them concerning Jesus, both from the Law of Moses and from the Prophets, from morning until evening.

**24** And some were persuaded by what was said, but others disbelieved.

**25** And being in disagreement with one another, they began to depart, after Paul had spoken one word: "Rightly did the Holy Spirit speak through Isaiah the prophet to your fathers,

**26** saying, 'Go to this people and say: By hearing you will hear and will by no means understand, and seeing you will see and will by no means perceive.

**27** For the heart of this people has grown dull, and with their ears they have heard heavily, and their eyes they have closed, lest they should see with their eyes and hear with their ears and understand with their heart and turn, and I would heal them.'

**28** Let it be known to you, therefore, that this salvation of God has been sent to the Gentiles; they indeed will listen."

**29** [And when he had said these things, the Jews departed, having much dispute among themselves.]

**30** And he remained two whole years in his own rented dwelling, and he welcomed all who came to him,

**31** proclaiming the kingdom of God and teaching the things concerning the Lord Jesus Christ with all boldness, unhindered.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

**Verse 1 — "Malta" (Melite):** The Sinaiticus reads MEAITH (Melite). This is consistently identified with modern Malta. The form "Malta" is used as the standard English rendering.

**Verse 2 — "native people" (barbaroi):** The Greek barbaroi does not carry the modern pejorative sense of "barbarian." It denotes those who do not speak Greek — the indigenous inhabitants. "Native people" preserves the descriptive, non-derogatory sense of the original.

**Verse 4 — "Justice" (dike):** Rendered with a capital letter as it personifies the Greek concept of divine retribution or the goddess Dike. The islanders speak from their own theological framework. The Sinaiticus reads H DIKH clearly.

**Verse 6 — "changed their minds" (metabalomenoi):** A strong term indicating a complete reversal of opinion. This is distinct from metanoia (repentance) and refers specifically to a change of judgment.

**Verse 11 — "Dioscuri" (Dioskourois):** The Twin Brothers, Castor and Pollux, sons of Zeus. Retained in transliterated form as a proper noun. These were patron deities of sailors; the ship's figurehead bore their image.

**Verse 14 — "And so we came to Rome":** The Sinaiticus places this clause at the end of verse 14 rather than as part of verse 15. This follows the manuscript's reading.

**Verse 16 — Sinaiticus omission:** Codex Sinaiticus omits the phrase about the centurion delivering the prisoners to the captain of the guard (found in some later manuscripts). The shorter reading is followed here as it represents the earliest manuscript witness.

**Verse 25 — "one word" (rhema hen):** That is, one final declaration — what follows is Paul's concluding prophetic pronouncement. The singular "word" carries the weight of a solemn utterance.

**Verses 26-27 — Isaiah 6:9-10:** Paul quotes from Isaiah, a passage that appears at pivotal moments throughout the New Testament (Matthew 13:14-15; John 12:40; Romans 11:8). The quotation follows the Septuagint text closely. The Sinaiticus preserves this passage with care.

**Verse 29 — Bracketed verse:** This verse is absent from the earliest and most reliable manuscripts, including Sinaiticus. It is included in brackets following scholarly convention, as it appears in later Byzantine manuscripts and in the Textus Receptus tradition.

**Verse 31 — "unhindered" (akolutos):** The final word of the Book of Acts. This adverb — placed emphatically at the end — captures Luke's theological purpose: despite imprisonment, trials, shipwreck, and opposition, the proclamation of the kingdom goes forward without obstruction. The word stands alone as a triumphant conclusion.

**Theological key terms maintained in this chapter:**
- "kingdom of God" (basileia tou theou) — consistent with prior Acts usage
- "the hope of Israel" (elpis tou Israel) — linking Paul's message to covenant promise
- "salvation of God" (soterion tou theou) — the full phrase, not abbreviated
- "Lord Jesus Christ" (kyrios Iesous Christos) — the complete Christological title at the close

--- LOGOS
